title: Health data caught in the geopolitical crossfire
html_body:
Biotechnology, drug discovery and pharma |
View this email in your browser Wednesday 29 April 2026

Hello Nature readers,
This week, we watch biologic degraders zip through the clinic, learn that the United States is speeding up research into psychedelics and hear from three gene therapy pioneers.
A number of biologic degraders are currently between phase I and III trials for diseases including the autoimmune disorder Graves’ disease and obesity. (S.Harris/Springer Nature Limited)

Biologic degraders zip through the clinic

A handful of antibody-based proteins that recruit a cell’s degradation machinery to target disease-linked proteins are advancing in the clinic. Biologic degraders join two other targeted protein degrader types — PROTACs and molecular glues — in a toolbox of similar drugs primarily targeting cancer and autoimmunity. “It’s really exciting to see all the momentum broadly in extracellular target protein degradation,” says biotechnology company head Steve Staben. But the field still needs to understand the limits of degraders’ abilities, notes biochemist Albert Lin.

Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 22 min read

US speeds research into psychedelics

US President Donald Trump signed an executive order to streamline research into psychedelic compounds and to make it easier for people with certain illnesses to access these drugs. It calls out by name the potent and long-lasting psychedelic ibogaine, which is something of a scientific mystery, in part because it is one of the most tightly controlled drugs in the United States. The move has been welcomed by researchers who see potential for drugs, including psilocybin and MDMA, in treating conditions such as addiction, depression and post-traumatic stress disorder. But scientists are also concerned about possible side effects, and the impact on health services if the drugs are approved.

Nature | 7 min read

Interview of the month
(Vivien Killilea/Getty Images for Breakthrough Prize)

Three gene therapy pioneers, and beekeeping

Jean Bennett, Albert Maguire and Katherine High discuss the journey to the approval of the first gene therapy in the United States, which won them the Breakthrough Prize in Life Sciences, and what’s next for them. “I recently retired,” says Maguire. “Now it’s painting and beekeeping — I’d advise against [it]. It’s extraordinarily frustrating. Everybody says how cool it is. You do not want to get into this.” (Endpoints News | 12 min read)

Features and opinion

Centre patients in drug development

“In an era in which precision medicine and personalized healthcare dominate discussion, the pharmaceutical industry must prioritize the voices of those it serves: patients,” write Allison Martin, regulation and policy lead at the drug company Sanofi, and Russ Paulsen, the head of the nonprofit UsAgainstAlzheimer’s. The approach should involve including data on quality of life in the drug development process, collected through surveys and interviews, “to bridge gaps between clinical endpoints and patient realities”. This approach is particularly crucial for neurodegenerative diseases, such as Alzheimer’s, where symptoms have profound emotional impacts on those afflicted and their caregivers, they note.

Nature Medicine | 7 min read

Health data caught in geopolitical crossfire

As scientists reel from the news that data from the influential UK Biobank system was found listed for sale on the Chinese e-commerce site Alibaba, Nature Medicine investigates how the geopolitics driving artificial intelligence superpowers is reshaping biomedical data sets, and who has access to them. Ultimately, says computational medicine researcher Alex Frangi, a balance must be struck between protecting national data and ensuring global inclusivity to ensure data are “a tool for universal clinical progress rather than a source of new health inequalities”.

BBC | 7 min read & Nature Medicine | 16 min read

Quote of the week

“Antibodies themselves are rather privileged structures, biologically.”

Medicinal chemist Derek Lowe outlines why the generally well-understood structure and the abundance of antibodies allows them to be good vessels for drugs and the most likely to be helped by AI in development. (Science | 6 min read)

Music heals too! Musician Jeff Beal credits playing music with helping to keep his multiple sclerosis under control.

Please send your favourite piano piece — and your feedback — to trbriefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Laura Woodrow, editor, Nature Briefing: Translational Research
With contributions by Flora Graham

ADVERTISEMENT FEATURE

Agata Kieliszek and Jakob Magolan are two of the McMaster researchers behind Block Biosciences, which is working on a first-in-class precision medicine that will prevent secondary metastatic brain cancer. (Georgia Kirkos/McMaster University )

Start-up aims to stop cancer spread

A McMaster University start-up is developing a new treatment to prevent cancer from spreading to the brain, an innovation that could revolutionize the future of cancer care. The Block Biosciences team is working on a new, first-in-class precision medicine that would be taken proactively by patients after a diagnosis of lung, breast or skin cancer, to prevent secondary metastatic brain cancer, which happens when cancer cells from another part of the body travel to the brain.

summary.overview: 이슈는 의료 데이터의 지정학적 갈등, 바이오 분해제의 임상 진입, 심리활성 물질 연구 가속화, 유전자 요법 개발자 인터뷰 등 과학 분야의 주요 이슈를 다룹니다.
covered_item_titles: Biologic degraders zip through the clinic
Three gene therapy pioneers, and beekeeping Jean Bennett, Albert Maguire and Katherine High discuss the journey to the approval of the first gene therapy in the United States
Health data caught in geopolitical crossfire

article_briefings.title: Biologic degraders zip through the clinic
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 항체 기반 단백질이 세포 분해 장치를 활용해 질병 관련 단백질을 표적으로 하는 바이오 분해제가 임상 단계로 진입하며, 이 분야의 혁신적인 접근법이 주목받고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: US speeds research into psychedelics
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 트럼프 대통령이 심리활성 물질 연구를 가속화하고 특정 질환 환자의 접근을 용이하게 하는 행정명령을 서명하며, ibogaine 등 강력한 심리활성 물질에 대한 연구가 주목받고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: Three gene therapy pioneers, and beekeeping Jean Bennett, Albert Maguire and Katherine High discuss the journey to the approval of the first gene therapy in the United States
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 유전자 요법 개발자인 제인 벤nett, 앨버트 마구어, 케이트린 하이가 미국 최초 유전자 요법 승인 과정을 공유하며, 연구자들의 후속 계획과 삶의 변화를 소개했습니다.

article_briefings.title: Health data caught in geopolitical crossfire
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 영국 빅뱅 데이터가 중국 알리바바에서 판매되는 것으로 드러나며, 인공지능 슈퍼파워 경쟁이 의료 데이터 보안과 글로벌 협력에 미치는 영향이 조사되고 있습니다.
